fact_text: Amersfort Place recalls Nieuw Amersfoort, the Dutch name for Flatlands before the British renamed it in 1664.
history_blurb: Amersfort Place takes its name from Amersfoort in the Dutch province of Utrecht. In 1636 Dutch settlers founded Nieuw Amersfoort — now Flatlands — as one of the five original Dutch towns on Long Island. The place itself is a leftover curve from the straightening of old Flatbush Road around 1890.

fact_text: Amherst Street starts Manhattan Beach's A-to-P alphabetical grid of English place names, laid out when the area was an exclusive 1870s seaside resort.
history_blurb: Amherst Street in Manhattan Beach belongs to an alphabetical series of streets with English place names running from A (Amherst) to P (Pembroke). The names were chosen when Manhattan Beach was developed as an exclusive seaside resort in the 1870s.

fact_text: Amity means 'friendship' in old English — and 197 Amity Street is where Winston Churchill's mother Jennie Jerome was born.
history_blurb: Amity Street takes its name from the English word for 'friendship.' The street was developed beginning in the 1840s with Greek Revival rowhouses. Its most notable address is 197 Amity Street — the birthplace of Jennie Jerome, who married Lord Randolph Churchill and became the mother of Winston Churchill.

fact_text: Argyle Road was plain old East 13th Street until developers renamed it — along with Rugby and Marlborough Roads — to give Flatbush a British air.
history_blurb: Argyle Road was originally East 13th Street, renamed in the early 1900s as part of the SWARM streets (Stratford, Westminster, Argyle, Rugby, Marlborough). The name comes from Argyll in western Scotland. Developers chose British place names to market the area as a refined residential enclave.

fact_text: Arion Place was renamed from Wall Street to honor Bushwick's famous German singing society — the 1886 hall is now loft apartments.
history_blurb: Arion Place was originally called Wall Street and was renamed for the Arion Mannerchor, a celebrated German men's chorus founded in 1867. The society took its name from Arion, a legendary Greek lyre player. The 1887 hall features lyre-shaped ironwork on its fire escapes and has since been converted to loft apartments.

fact_text: Arlington Avenue likely takes its name from Arlington, Virginia — the former Lee estate turned national cemetery after the Civil War.
history_blurb: Arlington Avenue runs through East New York and Cypress Hills. The name follows a common post-Civil War pattern of naming streets after Arlington, Virginia, which became nationally prominent after Robert E. Lee's estate was converted to Arlington National Cemetery in 1864.

fact_text: Ashland Place was once Raymond Street — renamed to shake the stigma of the notorious Raymond Street Jail that stood nearby.
history_blurb: Ashland Place was formerly Raymond Street, home to the infamous Raymond Street Jail that operated from the 1830s to 1963. The street was renamed to distance it from the jail's grim reputation. The name 'Ashland' likely honors Henry Clay's Kentucky estate, following a widespread 19th-century naming pattern.

fact_text: Autumn Avenue used to be Railroad Avenue, named for the nearby LIRR station before getting its seasonal rename.
history_blurb: Autumn Avenue runs from Jamaica Avenue to Liberty Avenue in East New York. It was formerly called Railroad Avenue, reflecting its proximity to the Long Island Rail Road station on Atlantic Avenue. The street was later given its current seasonal name as the neighborhood developed residentially.

fact_text: Baltic Street sits between Atlantic Ave and Pacific Street — all three named for bodies of water in Brooklyn's maritime heyday.
history_blurb: Baltic Street runs through Boerum Hill and Cobble Hill, part of a cluster of streets with maritime names including Atlantic Avenue and Pacific Street. The naming reflects Brooklyn's deep ties to shipping and waterfront commerce in the 19th century.

fact_text: Barbey Street is named for developer Andrew Barbey — his partner Sackman got a street too, about a mile to the west.
history_blurb: Barbey Street is named for Andrew Barbey of the 19th-century Barbey & Sackman real estate firm that helped build out East New York. His partner's name lives on in nearby Sackman Street. Between Sunnyside Avenue and Highland Boulevard, Barbey Street makes a dramatic S-curve up one of Brooklyn's steepest hills.

fact_text: Bath Avenue is named for Bath, England — developer B.J. Osborne wanted his 1850s seaside colony to feel just as refined.
history_blurb: Bath Avenue takes its name from Bath, Somerset, England, the town famous for its Roman-era hot springs. Developer B.J. Osborne purchased farmland along Gravesend Bay in the 1850s and established a genteel seaside resort modeled on the English spa town. The Brooklyn, Bath and Coney Island Railroad connected the resort starting in 1862.

fact_text: Battery Avenue is named for the gun batteries at Fort Hamilton — until 1898 locals could freely stroll right into the fort.
history_blurb: Battery Avenue takes its name from the artillery batteries at nearby Fort Hamilton, built in 1831 to guard the Narrows of New York Harbor. The public had open access to the fort until the Spanish-American War in 1898, when security concerns led to restricted access.

fact_text: Bayard Street honors the powerful Bayard family — Nicholas Bayard was Stuyvesant's nephew and became NYC mayor in 1685.
history_blurb: Bayard Street was formerly called Sandford Street and was renamed around 1870. It honors the prominent Bayard family of colonial New York. Nicholas Bayard (c. 1644-1707), nephew of Peter Stuyvesant, served as mayor of New York City from 1685-1686. The family became major landholders across the city.

fact_text: Beard Street honors William Beard, an Irish immigrant who built the Erie Basin and transformed Red Hook into a major shipping hub.
history_blurb: William Beard came to America in 1825 and made his fortune building railroads. Starting in 1843 he purchased waterfront land in Red Hook, filled in marshes, and built the Erie Basin — a man-made harbor that opened in 1864. Elizabeth Street was renamed Beard Street on December 14, 1865.

fact_text: The Beadels were local landowners connected to Peter Cooper, whose glue factory once dominated this part of Williamsburg.
history_blurb: Beadel Street is named for the Beadel family, who held large portions of land in the area during the mid-1850s. The family had connections to Peter Cooper, the prominent industrialist who operated a glue factory nearby — Cooper's two brothers married into the Beadel family.

fact_text: The Bedells were among Canarsie's founding families — descendants lived in the area from colonial times into the 1950s.
history_blurb: Bedell Lane is a quiet side street in Canarsie. The Bedells were one of Canarsie's earliest settler families, with Robert Bedell arriving from England in the 1600s. The family remained prominent in the area for centuries.

fact_text: Named for August Belmont, the Wall Street titan who founded the Belmont Stakes and changed his name from Schonberg to sound more French.
history_blurb: Belmont Avenue is named for August Belmont Sr., born in Germany as Aron Belmont. He began as an apprentice at the Rothschild banking house and became one of the wealthiest men in America. He chaired the Democratic National Committee for 12 years and founded the Belmont Stakes. The avenue became famous for its open-air pushcart market in Brownsville's immigrant era.

fact_text: Arthur W. Benson founded Brooklyn Gas, invested in the Brooklyn Bridge, and sold his farmland on the condition the neighborhood bear his name.
history_blurb: Benson Avenue takes its name from the Benson family whose farmland became Bensonhurst. Arthur W. Benson founded the Brooklyn Gas Light Company in 1823 and was one of only nine individual investors in the Brooklyn Bridge. He sold the land to developer James Lynch in the 1880s on the condition the neighborhood carry the Benson name.

fact_text: Named for philosopher George Berkeley — same guy Berkeley, California is named after. He pronounced it 'BARK-lee.'
history_blurb: Berkeley Place was formerly part of Sackett Street. In 1881, as Park Slope gentrified, residents petitioned for a more refined name. They chose George Berkeley, the Anglo-Irish philosopher and Bishop of Cloyne who pioneered immaterialism. The same figure inspired the naming of Berkeley, California.

fact_text: William Berriman was a newspaper man whose buddy, a local Democratic boss, got a Brooklyn street named after him.
history_blurb: Berriman Street was named for William Berriman of the Buffalo Evening News. The honor came through his friendship with Harry H. Adams, the Democratic leader of New Lots, who arranged the street naming.

fact_text: Originally just 'Avenue B,' developers gave it the posh English name Beverley in 1897 to sell more houses nearby.
history_blurb: Beverley Road was originally called Avenue B in Brooklyn's alphabetical grid south of Prospect Park. In 1897 the City of Brooklyn renamed it Beverley Road using the English spelling, after the town of Beverley in Yorkshire. Developer T.B. Ackerson was building Beverley Squares East and West nearby.

fact_text: Boerum Place honors Simon Boerum, a Brooklyn farmer turned Continental Congress delegate who helped defeat the Galloway Plan in 1774.
history_blurb: Boerum Place is named for Simon Boerum, whose family settled in Dutch New Netherland. He farmed and operated a mill in Flatbush before entering politics, serving as Kings County Clerk and delegate to the Continental Congress. He signed the Continental Association boycotting British goods.

fact_text: Boerum Street recalls Jacob Boerum, who farmed 58 acres in Williamsburg — his land was later sold to developers who built homes for German settlers.
history_blurb: Boerum Street in Williamsburg is named for Jacob Boerum, whose 58-acre farm lay in the Sixteenth Ward. The Boerum family's roots in Brooklyn trace to Willem Jacobse Van Boerum, who immigrated to New Amsterdam in 1649. Part of Jacob's farm was purchased and developed into homes for German settlers.

fact_text: Bogart Street is named for a 1650s Dutch settler — and yes, actor Humphrey Bogart was his direct descendant through seven generations.
history_blurb: Bogart Street is named for Teunis Gysbertse Bogaert, who arrived from the Netherlands in 1652 and married Sara Rapelje, said to be the first European girl born in New Netherland. He became one of Long Island's most prosperous farmers. Actor Humphrey Bogart was a direct descendant.

fact_text: Bond Street likely takes its name from Manhattan's Bond Street, itself possibly named for London's famous shopping street.
history_blurb: Bond Street in Brooklyn runs roughly a mile from Gowanus through Boerum Hill. Its naming likely echoes Manhattan's Bond Street, whose origin is debated — it may honor London's Bond Street in Mayfair or city surveyor Captain William Bond.

fact_text: Bradford Street hosted the New Lots Town Hall from 1873, which later became a police station and then a hospital for the neighborhood's poor.
history_blurb: Bradford Street runs through what was once the Town of New Lots, which seceded from Flatbush in 1852. The New Lots Town Hall was built at 109-111 Bradford Street in 1873. The building later housed a police precinct and then the Bradford Street Hospital, a walk-in clinic for the neighborhood's poor.

fact_text: Bragg Street is named for F. S. Bragg, one of the surveyors who literally drew the Sheepshead Bay street grid for Kings County in the 1870s.
history_blurb: Bragg Street honors F. S. Bragg, an engineer on the Kings County Town Survey Commission that laid out the streets of southern Brooklyn between 1869 and 1874. The cluster of named streets nearby — Bragg, Brigham, Batchelder, Coyle, Ford, Brown, Haring, and Knapp — all commemorate members of that same survey team.

fact_text: Brevoort Place recalls the Dutch Brevoort family, who took their name from the village of Bredevoort — meaning 'broad ford' — in the Netherlands.
history_blurb: Brevoort Place takes its name from the Brevoort family, whose ancestor Hendrick Janszen left the village of Bredevoort in the Netherlands around 1660 and adopted the place name as his surname. The Brevoorts became prominent Brooklyn landowners. J. Carson Brevoort maintained the family estate on Brevoort Place until 1875.

fact_text: Brigham Street is named for E. D. Brigham, a surveyor who mapped out this part of Sheepshead Bay's street grid in the 1870s.
history_blurb: Brigham Street honors E. D. Brigham, a surveyor on the Kings County Town Survey Commission that designed the street grid for southern Brooklyn between 1869 and 1874. Along with Bragg, Batchelder, Coyle, Ford, Brown, and Knapp, these streets replaced what would have been numbered avenues.

fact_text: Bristol Street was originally part of Baltic Street — renamed in 1887 when the New Lots Board of Trade reorganized 67 street names.
history_blurb: Bristol Street was created in an 1887 renaming when the New Lots Board of Trade recommended new names for 67 streets, each keeping the same initial letter as the original. Baltic Street became Bristol instead of the proposed Burton. The name likely references Bristol, England.

fact_text: Brooklyn Avenue is part of Crown Heights' Erie Canal tribute — avenues named in 1835 for cities along the canal route.
history_blurb: Brooklyn Avenue runs through Crown Heights as part of the named avenues platted in 1835 to honor cities along the Erie Canal. While neighboring avenues trace the canal from Kingston to Buffalo, Brooklyn Avenue honors the city of Brooklyn itself. By the late 1800s, wealthy merchants built mansions along the avenue near Eastern Parkway.

fact_text: Named for John Broome, a Revolutionary War militia colonel who became New York's lieutenant governor in 1804.
history_blurb: Broome Street honors John Broome, a Colonial-era merchant and politician who served as Lieutenant Governor of New York from 1804 until his death in 1810. Originally a Revolutionary War militia colonel under John Jay, Broome later became a prominent civic figure.

fact_text: Likely named for poet William Cullen Bryant, whose famous poem 'Thanatopsis' on death echoes through nearby Cypress Hills Cemetery.
history_blurb: Bryant Street is believed to honor William Cullen Bryant, the Romantic poet and longtime editor of the New York Evening Post. The street sits near Cypress Hills Cemetery, which features a path called Thanatopsis Way after Bryant's most famous poem — a meditation on death.

fact_text: Developer Dean Alvord gave this street its English name in 1899 to make his Prospect Park South subdivision feel like a country estate.
history_blurb: In 1899, developer Dean Alvord purchased about 60 acres of Flatbush farmland to create Prospect Park South with the motto 'rus in urbe' (country in the city). He renamed numbered streets with British names — Buckingham, Albemarle, Westminster, Rugby, Marlborough — to evoke an English garden suburb.

fact_text: Named in 1835 to honor the Erie Canal — seven Crown Heights avenues trace the canal route from Kingston to Buffalo.
history_blurb: Buffalo Avenue is one of seven Crown Heights avenues named in 1835 for New York State cities along the Erie Canal route. Running in geographic sequence — Kingston, Albany, Troy, Schenectady, Utica, Rochester, and Buffalo — they trace a boat's path from the Hudson River to Lake Erie.

fact_text: Named for the Dutch settlement 'Boswijck' — town in the woods — chartered by Peter Stuyvesant in 1661. It became a mansion-lined boulevard for brewers.
history_blurb: Bushwick Avenue takes its name from Bushwick, one of Brooklyn's six original Dutch towns, chartered by Governor Peter Stuyvesant in 1661 as 'Boswijck.' The land was purchased from the Canarsie Lenape in 1638. The avenue evolved into a showcase boulevard between 1880 and 1913, lined with mansions of German-American brewers.

fact_text: Part of Butler Street was renamed Sterling Place in 1873 after a clerk misspelled 'Stirling' — the old Butler name survives only in a few blocks.
history_blurb: Butler Street likely honors Benjamin Franklin Butler, the New York lawyer who served as U.S. Attorney General under Jackson and Van Buren and co-founded NYU. In 1873 the section between 5th Avenue and Flatbush was renamed Sterling Place for Lord Stirling, misspelled by a clerk.

fact_text: Butler Place kept the original name when most of Butler Street was renamed Sterling Place in 1873 — this stub survived the change.
history_blurb: Butler Place in Prospect Heights shares its origin with the longer Butler Street. When most of Butler Street was renamed Sterling Place between 1873 and 1897, this short section near Prospect Park retained the Butler name. The street sits within the Prospect Heights Historic District.

fact_text: Named for Vice President John C. Calhoun, who infamously called slavery a 'positive good' — many Calhoun streets nationwide have been renamed.
history_blurb: Calhoun Street is named for John C. Calhoun, who served as the 7th Vice President, Secretary of War, Secretary of State, and U.S. Senator. Calhoun was one of the most outspoken defenders of slavery in American politics. Many streets and institutions bearing his name have been renamed in recent years.

fact_text: Fort Greene's streets — Carlton, Adelphi, Cumberland, Portland — were all named after fashionable Westminster terraces in 19th-century London.
history_blurb: Carlton Avenue is part of a cluster of Fort Greene streets named after prestigious Westminster terraces from early 19th-century London. Carlton, Adelphi, Cumberland, Oxford, and Portland all reflect developers' aspirations during the 1850s-1860s building boom, when Fort Greene was one of Brooklyn's most prestigious neighborhoods.

fact_text: Named for the Caton family — Susan Martense Caton's mansion once stood right at Flatbush and Caton Avenues.
history_blurb: Caton Avenue honors the Caton family of old Flatbush. Susan Martense married Patrick Caton and inherited property including a house and mill at Flatbush and Caton Avenues. All three family names — Caton, Crooke, and Martense — appear on nearby Flatbush street signs.
